In this example, we explore how AI can support a common data science workflow:
taking raw data and transforming it into a clean, analysis-ready format.

Following the common agentic [data analysis workflow](/methods/common-workflow.qmd)
and principles of [safeguarding data](/methods/safeguard-data.qmd), we adopt a
**metadata-driven approach** — using a data dictionary as an intermediary between
the raw data and the AI, rather than exposing sensitive data directly.

By the end of this example, you will have practiced using AI to:

1. Explore the structure and content of a dataset.
2. Build a data dictionary that captures variable names, types, units, and labels.
3. Document data harmonization requirements systematically.
4. Generate harmonization code driven by metadata.
5. Iterate on that code to resolve errors and refine outputs.
6. Verify results and troubleshoot any remaining issues.

As examplary data we us Mexico National Survey of Household Income and
Expenditure (ENIGH) for 2016 and 2018, which contains detailed information on
household income, and other socio-economic characteristics.

This data was downloaded from:

- 2016: <https://en.www.inegi.org.mx/programas/enigh/nc/2016/> and
- 2018: <https://en.www.inegi.org.mx/programas/enigh/nc/2018/>

Metadata is available at:

- 2016: <https://www.inegi.org.mx/rnm/index.php/catalog/310>
- 2018:<https://www.inegi.org.mx/rnm/index.php/catalog/511>

This data is stored on your local machine on `OneDrive/.../ai4coding-data/mex/2016/`
as a read-only file.

The objective is to clean this data to the specifications describeed in the
global monitoring database (GMD) selectively outlines in [Self-Study -> GMD requirements.](/selfstudy/gmd-requirements.qmd). We would like to harmonize only following modules of the GMD:

1. Demographics
2. Income
3. Consumption
